title: Friday Sermon 14 June 2002
description:
This is the concluding sermon on the subject of the Divine attributes of al-Shaheed (The Witness) and al-Shahid (The Observer).  As ever elucidating the concept with reference to Quranic verses, the Ahadith and various writings 

The Holy Prophet (peace and blessings be on him) enjoined that the best witness is one who gives testimony even before it is requested:

Zaid bin Khalid al-Juhani said that he heard the Messenger of Allah say: The best of witnesses is the one who gives his testimony before he is asked for it.

(Abu Abdullah Muhammad ibn Majah.  'Sunan ibn Majah').

Ayesha narrated that the Messenger of Allah said: The testimony of a treacherous man is not acceptable nor a treacherous woman nor a man lashed for the Hadd nor a woman lashed nor one possessing malice of enmity nor a rehearsed witness nor that of one contracted by the family on their behalf nor the one associating himself to other than his Wala or to other than his relatives.

(Abu Isa Muhammad ibn Isa al-Tirmidhi.  'Jami al-Tirmidhi').

It is incumbent upon one to give a fair and truthful testimony for the sake of Allah even when it is against one’s parents and near and dear ones:

Be strict in observing justice and bear witness only for the sake of Allah, even if it should  occasion loss to you or your parents or your kinsmen or sons ....

(Hazrat Mirza Ghulam Ahmad.  'Islami Usul Ki Philosophy' ['The Philosophy of the Teachings of Islam'].  Matba Siddiqi, 1896, 1905.  'Ruhani Khazain: x'. 361.  English: Islam International Publications Ltd, 2010. 76).

One is enjoined to avoid the impurity of falsehood and make one’s testimony with justice being ever mindful of God, he added that Islam recognises the rights of the enemy in that despite one’s enmity, no matter how difficult it seems, one should treat one’s adversaries with utmost fairness.

(Ahmad.  'Nur al-Quran'.  Ziya al-Islam, 1895.  'R. K: ix'.  409). 

Explaining the doctrinal mention that man’s limbs will bear witness regarding his deeds:

Anas ibn Malik reported: We were in the company of Allah's Messengerﷺ when he smiled, and asked ‘Do you know why I laughed?’ We said ‘Allah and His Messenger, know best.’ Thereupon he said ‘It was because there came to my mind the talk which the servant would have with his Lord on the Day of Judgment.  He would ask ‘My Lord!  Have you not guaranteed me protection against injustice?’  He would reply ‘Yes.’  Then the servant would say ‘I do not deem valid any witness against me but my own self’ and He would say ‘Well, enough would be the witness of yourself against you and that of the two angels who had been appointed to record your deeds.’  Then the seal would be set upon his mouth and it would be said to his hands and feet to speak and they would speak of his deeds.  Then the mouth would be made free to talk, he would say to the hands and feet: Be away, let there be curse of Allah upon you.  It was for your safety that I contended’.

(Muslim ibn al-Hajjaj.  'Sahih Muslim').  
 
The Holy Quran bears witness that the Holy Prophet’s (peace and blessings on him) triumph over his opponents will be like the triumph of the Prophet Moses (on whom be peace) over his enemies.

(Hazrat al-Hajj Hakeem Maulana Nooruddin – Khalifatul Masih I.  'Tasdiq Barahin e Ahmadiyya’.  1890.  13, 14).

The Quran expounds the testimony of the affinity between the prophethood of the Holy Prophet (peace and blessings be on him) and that of the Prophet Moses (on whom be peace) in that the succession of the prophethood of Moses (on whom be peace) concluded upon Jesus Christ the Messiah (on whom be peace), who came 1400 years after Moses (on whom be peace) while divinely appointed vicegerents had established Moses’ (on whom be peace) testimony in the intervening period.  Accordingly it is evident that the Muhammadan Messiah should have come 1400 years after the Holy Prophet’s (peace and blessings be on him) advent to revive and re-establish the message of Islam.

(Ahmad.  8 January 1904.  'Malfuzat: iii'.  541, 542).
